coredata.dc:title: Preparation and dynamic color-changing study of fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for individualized and customized anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Weng, Yuchen
Hong, Ying
Deng, Jingyu
Cao, Sicheng
Fan, Li-Juan
coredata.dc:description: Preparing new fluorescent materials for individualized and customized anti-counterfeiting applications to meet needs from the rapid development of e-commerce is of great significance. This paper reports the preparation of dynamic color-changing fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) by constructing a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pair between aggregation-induced emission (AIE) structures and photochromic structures. At first, methyl methacrylate (MMA) was used as the main monomer and tetraphenylethylene (TPE, a typical AIE structure) modified methacrylate (TPE-MA) and photochromic spiropyran (SP) modified methacrylate (SP-MA) as minor monomers were copolymerized to obtain the ternary copolymer PMMA-TPE-SP. Then, two types of PNPs based on this terpolymer was prepared via the reprecipitation method, with and without the addition of an amphiphilic polymer as the surfactant. The photophysical study shows that the fluorescence color of PNPs dynamically changes from blue to light violet and finally to red under UV light irradiation, a process that can be reversed under visible light. The PNPs were alternately irradiated with UV light and visible light for 10 cycles, which proved their good photoswitching reproducibility. The PNPs prepared with addition of surfactant were found to have stronger fluorescence and better stability. Finally, the photochromic fluorescent inks were prepared based on these PNPs. Several anti-counterfeiting scenarios and modes were designed, exhibiting excellent photochromic behavior on cellulose paper, even after 120 days of long-term storage. With simple equipment, desirable anti-counterfeiting effects with dynamic fluorescence color changing was achieved. This study demonstrated a promising hard-to-imitate anti-counterfeiting encryption strategy, which can achieve multiple outputs with simple operation and can be personalized and customized as needed.

head.ce:title: Preparation and dynamic color-changing study of fluorescent polymer nanoparticles for individualized and customized anti-counterfeiting application

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Preparing new fluorescent materials for individualized and customized anti-counterfeiting applications to meet needs from the rapid development of e-commerce is of great significance. This paper reports the preparation of dynamic color-changing fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) by constructing a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) pair between aggregation-induced emission (AIE) structures and photochromic structures. At first, methyl methacrylate (MMA) was used as the main monomer and tetraphenylethylene (TPE, a typical AIE structure) modified methacrylate (TPE-MA) and photochromic spiropyran (SP) modified methacrylate (SP-MA) as minor monomers were copolymerized to obtain the ternary copolymer PMMA-TPE-SP. Then, two types of PNPs based on this terpolymer was prepared via the reprecipitation method, with and without the addition of an amphiphilic polymer as the surfactant. The photophysical study shows that the fluorescence color of PNPs dynamically changes from blue to light violet and finally to red under UV light irradiation, a process that can be reversed under visible light. The PNPs were alternately irradiated with UV light and visible light for 10 cycles, which proved their good photoswitching reproducibility. The PNPs prepared with addition of surfactant were found to have stronger fluorescence and better stability. Finally, the photochromic fluorescent inks were prepared based on these PNPs. Several anti-counterfeiting scenarios and modes were designed, exhibiting excellent photochromic behavior on cellulose paper, even after 120 days of long-term storage. With simple equipment, desirable anti-counterfeiting effects with dynamic fluorescence color changing was achieved. This study demonstrated a promising hard-to-imitate anti-counterfeiting encryption strategy, which can achieve multiple outputs with simple operation and can be personalized and customized as needed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the rapid development of modern science and technology not only brings great convenience to human beings, but also makes it possible for counterfeiters to produce fake goods or documents at a relatively low cost, thus bringing great harm to society. For example, counterfeit food and medicine can pose very serious health risks to consumers[1,2]; the existence of fake money or valuable items not only harm personal interests but may also disturb the order of the world economy[3]. Over the past few decades, a wide variety of anti-counterfeiting techniques have been developed[4,5]. However, counterfeiters may already be familiar with the commonly used anti-counterfeiting methods and can imitate them. Hence, there is always a need to develop new anti-counterfeiting and identification technologies. Fluorescence (FL) based anti-counterfeiting has attract continuous attention due to the advantages of fast deciphering, simple operation, and diverse application scenarios. Upconversion nanocrystals[6], organic small molecule fluorescent dyes[7], carbon dots[8–10] and fluorescent conjugated polymers[11] have been used to construct fluorescent anti-counterfeiting systems; most of them require a simple operation for realizing a single response pattern. In general, besides simple operation for deciphering/verification, fabrication with relatively cheap materials, encryption with relatively complex mechanism to avoid imitation, are also preferred in anti-counterfeiting applications. Therefore, the development of anti-counterfeiting materials with dynamic multicolor fluorescence changes by combining several different modes of fluorescence modulation has become a new trend in this field[12–17].

There are several ways for achieving dynamically tuning fluorescence. Photochromic materials have been used for anti-counterfeiting due to their dynamic color-changing feature under UV light irradiation[18–21]. Spiropyran (SP) is a popular photochromic organic molecule[22], having a non-planar ring-closed form which is non-fluorescent; UV light irradiation can convert SP into a ring-open form with a plane of conjugation, known as merocyanine (MC), which emits fluorescence. MC can revert to SP under external stimuli, such as visible light irradiation, heat or pressure. In addition, the introduction of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) donor–acceptor pairs is also a common method for tuning fluorescence color. Researchers are keen to introduce SP (actually MC) as a fluorescent acceptor for FRET to prepare various anti-counterfeiting materials, such as directly used as anti-counterfeiting labels in the form of thin films[23–25]. Developing more forms of dynamic fluorescent tunable materials may provide more options for anti-counterfeiting technologies to meet different application needs.

Recently, polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) have been often prepared as anti-counterfeiting inks due to their excellent water dispersibility and photostability[11,13,15,26]. PNPs containing SP molecules or moieties, have been widely used for various applications, such as bioimaging[27], drug delivery[28,29], and also anti-counterfeiting[15]. A few researcher groups have reported the preparation of PNPs with SP as the FRET donor for counterfeiting applications, most of the related studies prepared PNPs via emulsion polymerization[30], but only very a few via reprecipitation method[31]. In addition, to our best knowledge, very few have carried out detailed photophysical investigation on the photochromic properties of nanoparticles[32], even fewer on the repeatability of the photoswitching process to give an evaluation of the fatigue properties of the nanoparticles as anti-counterfeiting materials[31]. Therefore, if such repeatability studies can be carried out and correlated with molecular structure, we can better understand the photochromic properties of nanoparticles, making the preparation and application of such nanoparticles more controllable to meet the requirements of specific anti-counterfeiting applications.

In order to construct a FRET system, there must be as much overlap as possible between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor, so we need to select spectrally matched fluorophore (as the donor) for MC (as the acceptor)[33]. The fluorescence of nanoparticles containing conventional fluorescent dyes may sometimes be attenuated or even completely lost due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). Employing aggregation-induced emission (AIE) molecules may circumvent above problems[34]. PNPs containing AIE molecules have been used as multifunctional nanocarriers for a wide range of biological applications[35], but to our knowledge, relatively few studies have so far combined photochromic PNPs with AIE molecules for photoswitching dynamic color-changing fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks[36].

Here, we propose a strategy to construct a photoswitching dynamic color-changing fluorescent system by covalently introducing the photochromic spiropyran (SP) and the AIE molecules onto a polymer backbone, via a radical copolymerization. Tetraphenylethylene (TPE) is a typical AIE fluorescent molecule; which emits only faint or almost no fluorescence in a good solvent while displays significantly enhanced fluorescence in solid or aggregated state in which intramolecular rotation of different parts of the molecule is restricted [34]. Due to the large area of overlap between the fluorescence spectrum of TPE and the absorption spectrum of MC, TPE was chosen as the donor in the FRET system in this study. To successfully construct a FRET system, in addition to the prerequisite for spectral overlap described above, the proximity between donor and acceptor is also a key factor [33]. Small molecule-based system for constructing FRET pair between TPE and MC has been reported[37]. However, according to our previous study, compared with simple blending, covalent linkage between conjugated polymer backbone (donor) and MC (acceptor) as the pedant group should be more advantageous to achieve effective FRET and obvious color changing, both in poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) matrix[25]. Therefore, here we will synthesize a photochromic polymer, a TPE and SP modified poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA-TPE-SP), which is expected to have photoswitching fluorescence (Scheme 1
a) in an appropriate condition, then PNPs are to be prepared via reprecipitation method (Scheme 1 b). Finally, fluorescent inks are prepared based on the above PNPs, and it is hoped that the dynamic color-changing fluorescence anti-counterfeiting function can be realized through the combination of photochromic and FRET.

Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC), 2,3,3-trimethylindolenine, 5-nitrosalicylaldehyde, 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), 2,2′-azobis (2-methyl propionitrile) (AIBN) and 2-bromoethanol were purchased from energy-chemical Co., Ltd. 5-nitrososalicylaldehyde and 4-hydroxybenzophenone were purchased from Shanghai Haohong Biopharmaceutical Technology Co., Ltd. Methyl methacrylate (MMA), methacrylic acid (MAA), and benzophenone were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. Poly(styrene-co-Maleic anhydride) (PSMA,
M n
¯
∼1900) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Zinc was purchased from Damas-beta. Titanium tetrachloride was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Other commercially available reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ultra dry N, N-Dimethylformamide was purchased from Yonghua Chemical Co., Ltd. All reagents and solvents were of analytical grade and used without any purification unless otherwise specified.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu RF-5301 spectrofluorometer with excitation set at 365 nm. The 1H NMR spectra were obtained from a Vnmrs-300 MHz NMR spectrometer. The FTIR spectra were obtained on a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrophotometer. The elemental analyses were carried out on Carlo-Erba Elemental Analyzer EA 1110. Gel permeation chromatography (GPC) was measured by Waters 1515 system with polystyrene as the standard and THF as the mobile phase. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on a Brookhaven BI-200SM setup equipped with a 22 mW He–Ne laser (λ = 633 nm), BI-200SM goniometer, and BI-TurboCorr digital correlator. The mean hydrodynamic diameter (Dh) and particle size distribution (PDI) were evaluated by DLS. The accuracy range of the DLS instrument is from 10 nm to 5 μm. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on a Transmission Electron Microscope HT7700 (HT7700, Hitachi company, Japan); and the samples for TEM were prepared by dropping 8 μL of developing solution onto a copper grid and then freeze-drying. Photoluminescence quantum yield (Φf) of PNPs was determined by setting excitation at 365 nm and using quinine sulfate as standard (Φf = 0.546 in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, excited at 365 nm). The quantum yields reported here were the average values after 3 repeated measurements. The UV light irradiation was carried out using a UV lamp (WFH-204B, 365 nm, 12 W or FLSH-X-7, 365 nm, 60 W) and the visible light irradiation was carried out using a multi-band light source flashlight (SX-S18, white, 5 W). Photographs and fluorescent images were obtained with a Nikon D5100 camera under natural light or 365 nm UV excitation. All fluorescent patterns and drop-coated papers were applied on Whatman Qualitative Filter Paper (Cat. No. 1001–125).

1-(4-Hydroxyphenyl)-1,2,2-triphenylethene (TPE-OH) and 2-(3′,3′-dimethyl-6-nitrospiro[chromene-2,2′-indolin]-1′-yl)ethan-1-ol (SP-OH) were synthesized according to the literatures[38,39]. The synthesis of TPE-MA and SP-MA were shown in Scheme S1.

PMMA-TPE-SP. The pre-dried Schlenk flask was deoxygenated via three cycles of vacuum and argon. Under the protection of argon atmosphere, TPE-MA (0.073 g, 0.174 mmol), SP-MA (0.03 g, 0.07 mmol), methyl methacrylate (0.328 g, 3.234 mmol), AIBN (0.024 g, 0.14 mmol), and ultra dry DMF (3.0 mL) were added into the flask. The mixture was stirred for 30 min at room temperature, heated to 75 °C for 24 h, and then cooled to room temperature. The crude product was diluted by adding a small amount of dichloromethane (5 mL). Afterwards, the solution was slowly poured into 150 mL of methanol to give precipitate, which was centrifuged and dried in vacuum to produce a light-yellow powder (0.18 g, yield: 42.00 %). 1H NMR (300 MHz, Chloroform-d) δ 8.04 (s, 3H), 7.27 – 6.59 (m, 58H), 5.93 (s, 1H), 4.10 (s, 2H), 4.00 – 3.18 (m, 108H). = 20.8 kDa; PDI = 1.86.

PNPs prepared based on PMMA-TPE-SP solution (PMTS PNPs). PMTS PNPs were prepared by using a slightly modified reprecipitation method. In a typical procedure, a tetrahydrofuran (THF) solution containing 1 mg/mL of PMMA-TPE-SP was prepared. Then, 1 mL of the solution was rapidly injected into 10 mL of water under intense sonication ultrasound, and the mixture was sonicated for 5 min. Above mixture was placed at 40 °C in vacuum (21 kPa) to remove THF to give an aqueous solution of PMMA-TPE-SP (about 0.1 mg/mL) PNPs for future study.

PNPs prepared based on the mixed solution of PSMA and PMMA-TPE-SP (PMTS/PSMA PNPs). PMTS/PSMA PNPs were prepared by a procedure similar to the method described above. First, a mixture of PSMA and PMTS/PSMA PNPs was prepared by thoroughly mixing 2.5 mL of pre-prepared PMMA-TPE-SP solution (2 mg/mL, THF) and 0.625 mL of PSMA (2 mg/mL, THF) and making up the volume of the above solution to 5 mL. Then, using the same reprecipitation operation as above to give an aqueous solution with the same concentration of PMMA-TPE-SP (0.1 mg/mL) as the stock solution of PMTS/PSMA PNPs for future study. Besides, the concentration of PSMA in PMTS/PSMA is 0.025 mg/mL.

The photoquenching efficiency of PNPs was calculated according to Equation. 1.
(1)
E q
=
1 -
I
I 0
× 100 %
where Eq is the photoquenching efficiency, I and I0 are the fluorescence intensity of TPE at 460 nm, from the emission spectra recorded on days 0, 7, 14, 21, and 30, after 1 min UV light irradiation and before irradiation, respectively. The specific operation for recording the spectra is as follows: 3 mL of PNPs in a colloidal solution in a covered containers was stored in the dark at room temperature for one month, during that time the solution in the container was taken out on days 0, 7, 14, 21, and 30 for fluorescence spectroscopy measurement and then immediately returned to its original container.

The preparation of the ink in this study was modified from the literature[11,31]. Briefly, the above PNPs (about 0.1 mg/mL) were concentrated to a solution with a concentration of about 2 mg/mL, which was realized by using an Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter on a low-speed centrifuge at a speed of 4000 rpm for 10 min which was repeated several times. Then Triton X-100 (about 0.05 wt%) was added to the aqueous solution. Mixing 250 μL of glycerol and 75 μL of ethanol with 1 mL of above concentrated PNPs solution (about 2 mg/mL) gave a mixture that can be directly used for various anti-counterfeiting applications.

To improve the compatibility of different components in the system, methyl methacrylate (MMA) is chosen as the main monomer, since the rich ester group structure in MMA is beneficial to improve the stability of MC[25]. On the other hand, TPE and SP is to be covalently introduced into methacrylic acid (MAA) as synthesized according to Figure S1 to give TPE-MA and SP-MA monomers, respectively. Subsequently, free radical copolymerization is to be carried out with MMA with adding small amount of TPE-MA and SP-MA to give PMMA-TPE-SP. We expect small impact on the on the MMA polymerization process because only small amount of TPE-/SP-modified methacrylate monomers is added, and their reactivities is likely to be similar to that of MMA in this copolymerization. Thus, we hope that random copolymerization is possible to give copolymers having composition similar to the feed ratio of comonomers. Therefore, we hope that by random copolymerization, a copolymer with a composition similar to the feed ratio of the comonomers can be obtained.

It is worth emphasizing that TPE molecules do not emit significant fluorescence in an unrestricted state, for example when they dissolve well in solution[40]. To achieve fluorescence color change, it should be ensured that the TPE units in the copolymer can emit significant fluorescence, which requires that the copolymer be in a state of restricted intramolecular motion, such as a solid or aggregated states. Polymer nanoparticles (PNPs) usually consist of hydrophobic chains as the core and hydrophilic chains as the shell. Photochromic PNPs are prepared by incorporating photochromic compounds into the hydrophobic inner core of PNPs via simple doping[14,41] or covalent introduction, like copolymerization[42] and post-polymerization modification. Among them, covalent introduction is advantageous over physically doping, as they are expected to have better performance in fatigue resistance and reversibility after alternating irradiation of UV and visible lights. In addition, the precipitation of the polymer chains into nanoparticles can provide a solid micro-environment in the core to restrict intramolecular motion of TPE so that they can fluoresce.

To our knowledge, there is a report about the preparation of nanoparticles by emulsion polymerization of TPE and SP on a polymer backbone with butyl acrylate (BA) and MMA[36], where “polymer matrix modulated fluorescence technique” was proposed for the design and optimization of fluorescent materials for anti-counterfeiting applications. In their study, the nanoparticles prepared by emulsion polymerization were used directly, but the structure and photophysics of the polymers themselves were not characterized. In our study, to better understand the relationship between the chemical structure and photophysics of the polymer, we first synthesize and purify the polymer, and then perform comprehensive characterization on its chemical structure and study the photophysics of the polymer in solution. The above polymer is then prepared into nanoparticles via reprecipitation method and further investigation about these nanoparticles is to be carried out, especially the photoswitching reproducibility.

To investigate whether the addition of surfactant can improve the stability of nanoparticles in water and affect the fluorescence performance, two types of polymer nanoparticles were prepared in parallel, namely, PMTS PNPs or PMTS/PSMA PNPs, with the same copolymer PMMA-TPE-SP but without or with of the amphiphilic surfactant poly (styrene-maleic anhydride) (PSMA).Then their photophysical properties are to be compared and the effect of surfactants on the properties of PNPs is to be investigated. Finally, we hope with the comprehensive understanding of the properties of PNPs, the anti-counterfeiting application of these materials can be optimized to the maximum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization, and photophysical study of the polymer in solution
ce:section.ce:para: TPE-MA and SP-MA were synthesized by esterification of methacrylic acid with TPE-OH and SP-OH, respectively, which were synthesized according to the literature[38,39], as shown in Scheme S1. Then, AIBN-initiated radical polymerization of TPE-MA, SP-MA, and MMA produced PMMA-TPE-SP (Scheme 2 ). The chemical structures of monomers and polymers were characterized by 1H NMR and FTIR, and the molecular weight of the polymers was measured by GPC. The original spectra of 1H NMR of monomers and polymers are shown in Figure S1, S2 and S3 ; the stacked 1H NMR spectra of TPE-MA, SP-MA, MMA, and PMMA-TPE-SP are shown in Fig. 1 with all peaks labeled for the corresponding protons, which is consistent with the expected chemical structure. The proton signals at 6.30 ppm and 5.72 ppm in the spectrum of TPE-MA and at 6.08 ppm and 5.57 ppm in the spectrum of SP-MA correspond to the characteristic peaks of terminal olefin hydrogen (C=CH2) in the two monomers, respectively, which disappear in the spectrum of the polymer PMMA-TPE-SP, indicating successful polymerization. The 1H NMR spectrum is shown in detail in Figure S3 , followed by a relevant discussion. The final composition of the polymer was calculated to be SP-MA: TPE-MA: MMA = 3.08:6.47:90.45 (molar ratio), which was similar to the actual feeding ratio (2:5:93). Although the ratio of SP to TPE was slightly higher, it was within the acceptable error range, and we therefore said that the expected random copolymerization was achieved. The FTIR spectra are shown in Figure S4 . The absorption peaks of TPE-MA and SP-MA at 3100–3000 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of CH in vinyl disappeared in the spectrum of the polymer PMMA-TPE-SP, further proving the successful polymerization. According to the GPC curve ( Figure S5 ), the number-average molecular weight of PMMA-TPE-SP is 11.2 kDa, and the weight-average molecular weight is 20.8 kDa with a polydispersity index of 1.86. In conclusion, it is known from the above characterization that the polymer is in line with our expectations.

The photophysics of PMMA-TPE-SP in THF was studied by collecting the absorption and emission spectra of PMMA-TPE-SP after different time of UV irradiation (Fig. 2
a). As seen from the absorption spectra, with the increase of the irradiation time, a new absorption band between 475 nm and 675 nm appeared and then become higher and higher, which can be attributed to the isomerization of SP pendants on the polymer under UV light irradiation to generate merocyanine (MC) [25]. From the pictures of the polymer solution under natural light, we can see that the initial solution of the polymer is colorless and transparent, turning blue-violet after UV light irradiation, which is consistent with the change in absorption spectrum. However, as seen from the emission spectra with the excitation wavelength set at 530 nm for exciting MC fluorophore, only a very weak and low signal-to-noise emission peak, around 650 nm corresponding to the emission of MC, appeared upon 365 nm UV light irradiation and then slightly enhanced[43]. So, it is reasonable that no fluorescence is observed from the polymer solution under 530 nm green light, both before and after 365 nm UV light irradiation, as shown in the inset. As also discussed in the literature, lacking significant emission from MC is very likely due to that the unique inner salt hydrophilic structure of MC which affects the stability of the planar MC structure as a fluorophore in THF[25,44,45]; in addition, the presence of ester pendant group in the polymer still is not enough to stabilize it. In all, above study demonstrated the conversion from SP to MC can be achieved upon UV light irradiation of PMMA-TPE-SP in THF, but significant macroscopic photochromism cannot be observed.

As a typical AIE structure, TPE is usually non-fluorescent when dissolved in a good solvent such as THF, but exhibits blue fluorescence when aggregated. To investigate whether PMMA-TPE-SP also has an AIE effect, the emission spectra of the polymer in the mixed solvent, THF/H2O were recorded. Note that here the excitation was set at 365 nm to excite the TPE in aggregated state. As shown in Fig. 2 b, starting with pure THF, no fluorescence emission is observed; and the fluorescence emission near 460 nm appeared and then gradually increased by increasing the ratio of H2O in the mixed solvent, reaching the strongest when the H2O content is 90 % among the mixed solvents tested. Thus, the polymer PMMA-TPE-SP has a typical AIE characteristics. Interestingly, a very weak emission around 650 nm also appears and the peak kept rising when increasing water content, reaching its maximum at 80 % water content. Very likely, the fluorescence emission around this wavelength can be attributed to the superposition of the tail of TPE emission with the MC emission. This peak slightly decreases at 90 % water content, probably due to the decrease in polymer solubility with increasing water content. However, the emission around 650 nm is very weak compared with the emission around 460 nm, suggesting that a 365 nm excitation light source can also result in a small degree of SP-to-MC conversion, and FRET between TPE and MC in mixed solvents also occurs albeit to a low extent. In all, when changing the solvent from a good solvent to a poor solvent, the TPE moiety in PMMA-TPE-SP exhibits typical AIE behavior.

To summarize, UV light irradiation successfully induced SP-to-MC conversion of PMMA-TPE-SP in THF, and the typical AIE behavior of PMMA-TPE-SP was observed in mixed solvents. The above photophysical studies gave us a good understanding of the polymer itself, which will help us in the subsequent study of nanoparticle systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the polymer nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, the PMMA-TPE-SP polymer did not achieve dynamic fluorescence color changes in THF solution, duo to the lack of restriction of intramolecular motion of TPE unit and the low stability of MC with hydrophilic inner salt structure in solution. Encapsulating SP and TPE into nanoparticles can provide a solid-like microenvironment, and thus above problems may be solved. Here, two types of polymer nanoparticles were prepared by a modified reprecipitation method, one is based on PMMA-TPE-SP without adding any surfactant (PMTS PNPs) and another is prepared by blending PMMA-TPE-SP with amphiphilic surfactant PSMA in the initial solution (PMTS/PSMA PNPs), and the detailed preparation procedure is described in the experimental section. Note that the concentration of PNPs used in the subsequent particle size characterization and spectroscopic measurements is 0.1 mg/mL. PSMA is an amphiphilic copolymer that is often used as a surfactant in the preparation of polymeric nanoparticles. Like most surfactants, PSMA works to improve the solubility of hydrophobic polymers in water and to make the nanoparticles smaller in size [11,31]. In general, the fluorescence properties of fluorescent nanoparticles, such as fluorescence intensity, may be affected by the size of the particles; reduction in size may reduce the scattering effect, thereby enhancing the fluorescence intensity of the particles.[46] Therefore, the addition of the PSMA may produce nanoparticles with relatively strong fluroescence. Furthermore, due to the nature of polymer, PSMA should be more stable on nanoparticles than those small molecule surfactants that are prone to leakage. We hope that under such experimental conditions, fluorescent nanoparticles with good stability and relatively strong intensity can be obtained.

DLS and TEM characterizations. The average hydrodynamic diameters of PMTS and PMTS/PSMA PNPs in aqueous colloidal solution were found to be about 47 nm and 26 nm, respectively, by DLS measurements and had a narrow particle size distribution (Fig. 3
a). The TEM images of the PNPs obtained by lyophilizing the colloidal solution (Fig. 3 b and c) show that the PNPs are spherical, and the enlarged image in the upper-right inset shows that their average diameters match with the DLS data. In conclusion, nanoparticles with particle size of tens of nanometers were successfully prepared by simple reprecipitation method, and the addition of amphiphilic polymeric surfactant during the preparation process could further reduce the particle size.

Photochromic Properties Study of PNPs. To examine if the PNPs have the desired photophysics, the emission spectra of the nanoparticles upon irradiation of UV light and then visible light for different time were recorded. Theoretically, the use of 365 nm as the excitation wavelength to obtain the emission spectrum should be avoided, as it may also induce SP-to-MC conversion. However, we still chose 365 nm for excitation, which is based on several considerations. First, 365 nm can excite the emission of TPE in rotation-restricted state. Second, the 365 nm is a common wavelength for UV lamp available and fluorescence spectra obtained with excitation same as the actual application will be helpful for us to understand the principle for anti-counterfeiting. Last, this conversion cannot be at very high level because the excitation is usually completed in a very short time[46]. Here we use relatively low-power light for irradiation, 12 W UV light lamp as the UV light source and 5 W white light flashlight as the visible light source, based on consideration of making the evolution of the spectra as well as the macroscopic dynamic color change easy to track.

As shown in Fig. 4
a for the emission spectra of PMTS/PSMA PNPs, initially (t = 0 s), TPE units in the nanoparticles exhibit strong emission around 460 nm, and emission from MC around 650 nm also appears. Then, as the UV light exposure time increases, the emission at 460 nm gradually decreases, and the emission around 650 nm increases with a slight redshift (within 10 nm). Several conclusions can be made based on above observation. First, encapsulating TPEs into the nanoparticles achieved the restriction of intramolecular rotation; second, the 365 nm excitation light also induced the SP-to-MC conversion, and then FRET between TPE and MC occurred even at the very beginning (t = 0 s); third, subsequent further irradiation with 365 nm UV light led to the formation of more MC structures, which further reduced the fluorescence intensity corresponding to the TPE unit and enhanced the MC emission. After PMTS/PSMA PNPs were irradiated with 365 nm UV light for 5 min, the PNPs were continued to irradiate with visible light and the emission spectra were recorded after different time of visible light irradiation (Fig. 4 b). The emission around 460 nm gradually increased with the increase of visible light irradiation time accompany with the decrease of the emission around 650 nm. This observation proves that MC-to-SP conversion occurs under visible light irradiation, so that there is less FRET from TPE structure to MC structure, resulting in the recovery of TPE fluorescence emission. Figure S6 shows the emission spectra of PMTS PNPs upon irradiation of UV and visible lights, which changes with a trend similar to that of PMTS/PSMA PNPs. In addition, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the PMTS PNPs and PMTS/PSMA PNPs at different time of UV light (365 nm) irradiation are also similar to those of PNPs in THF solution ( Figure S7 and S8 ). In summary, SP-to-MC conversion, and subsequently FRET from TPE to MC were achieved for both PNPs.

To provide a macroscopic view of the dynamic fluorescence response, the color change process under continuous UV light irradiation was videotaped. Fig. 4 c shows the images intercepted from the video at different time of irradiation. With the increase of UV light irradiation time, the fluorescent color of PNPs gradually changed from blue to light violet and finally to red. It is worth noting that the macroscopic apparent fluorescence color is actually the mixed color of TPE (blue) and MC (red) in different proportions so that the fluorescence emission of the intermediate state (light violet) appears. In conclusion, the PNPs exhibited significant dynamic photochromism that can be easily observed with the naked eye, which is in good agreement with the fluorescence emission spectra.

To study the photoconversion process of above two types PNPs in more detail, the UV–visible absorbance at 570 nm (from MC), and the emission intensities at 460 nm (from TPE) and 650 nm (from MC) were plot against the irradiation time of UV light and visible light (Fig. 5
a, b, and c); these data were from Fig. 4 a, 4b, S6, S7 and S8. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the absorbance of MC in the two PNPs increased rapidly at first with the increase of UV light irradiation time, then slowed down and finally stabilized after 5 min of irradiation; after the exposure of visible light, the absorbance is also reduced rapidly and then slowly until it stabilized after 8 min, and almost completely returned to the initial state before UV light irradiation. Above result demonstrated the reversible conversion between SP and MC. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the change of emission intensity of TPE displays a opposite trend to that of the absorbance of MC, first rapidly decreasing under UV light, then slowly declining and finally stabilizing; after changing the light to visible light, the intensity rapidly increasing and leveling off. Then as shown in Fig. 5 c, the change in emission intensity of MC is very similar to the change in the absorbance of MC. These observations reveal that the conversion between SP and MC affects the degree of FRET between TPE and MC, i.e., the more SP units converted to the MC, the higher degree of FRET that occurs, resulting in less TPE emission and more MC emission.

Interestingly, although PMTS/PSMA PNPs and PMTS PNPs showed similar trends, PMTS/PSMA PNPs had higher MC absorbance, higher emission intensities of TPE and MC most of the time, and a larger difference between minimum and maximum values. In addition, the overall fluorescence quantum yield of PMTS/PSMA PNPs was found to be 3.56 %, higher than that of PMTS PNPs (1.51 %). Such difference can also be observed on the macroscopic view. As shown in Fig. 5 d, the PMTS PNPs and PMTS/PSMA PNPs shows similar color change, from initial blue state to intermediate light violet state and finally red state, but the apparently the fluorescence of PMTS/PSMA PNPs is much stronger; note here 60 W UV light was used for stimulate the transition of the fluorescence color more quickly, so the fluorescence color of the intermediate state differs compared with the color observed using UV light (12 W). There are several explanations may account for the differences observed above. First, PMTS/PSMA PNPs are smaller in size and thus the polymer chains are more closely aggregated; in this way, MC have more contacts with the ester groups inside the particles, which makes them more stable as chromophores, displaying stronger absorbance, as shown in Fig. 5 a. Second, the smaller size also causes a higher degree of restriction of the intramolecular motion of the TPE units, resulting in stronger TPE fluorescence in PMTS/PSMA PNPs (Fig. 5 b). Finally, as reported in the literature, the smaller size of fluorescent nanoparticles, the smaller the scattering effect, resulting in greater fluorescence intensity[46]. The reason for the higher MC emission of PMTS/PSMA PNPs in Fig. 5 c can be explained in conjunction with Fig. 5 a and 5b, that is, in the TPE-MC FRET pair, the stronger the fluorescence of the donor (TPE) and the higher the absorbance of the acceptor (MC), the more efficient the FRET process is and thus the stronger fluorescence of the acceptor (MC). In addition, in Fig. 5 b, the fluorescence intensity of TPE in both PNPs eventually decreases to the same minimum level after a certain period of UV light irradiation, which can also be attributed to the overall effect of the more effective FRET canceling out the stronger TPE fluorescence. In summary, most of the time, the emission intensities from TPE and MC in the PMTS/PSMA PNPs are both stronger than those in PMTS PNPs, so the mixed fluorescence of TPE and MC appears brighter on the macroscopic level.

Fatigue Resistance and Long-term Stability Study on the Photochromic Behavior. Regardless of the field of application, it is always necessary to examine the fatigue-resistance and long-term stability of the material. Based on the consideration of the counterfeiting application that may require multiple verification, such study about the photochromic behavior the nanoparticle will be examined by irradiating the PNPs in colloidal solution with alternating UV light and visible light for 10 cycles. Then the fluorescence spectrum after each irradiation was recorded. Prior to this, the light exposure time for subsequent reversible studies was determined according to Fig. 5 a and b. After irradiation with UV light (365 nm), the fluorescence intensity of TPE (460 nm) is sharply quenched by about 90 % within 1 min, then stabilized within 5 min (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence intensity almost stabilized after 10 min of visible light irradiation (5 W), and most of the fluorescence intensity (60 %) can be recovered within the first 5 min (Fig. 5 b). The above results correspond well in the MC fluorescence intensity trend plot in Fig. 5 c. Note that the visible light source used for irradiation in this study was a commercially available low-power (5 W) white-light flashlight. Therefore, the irradiation times of UV light (12 W) and visible light (5 W) were determined to be 1 min and 5 min, respectively, for the following fatigue resistance study.

From the emission spectra of PMTS/PSMA PNPs recorded for 10 cycles of alternating irradiation of UV light (1 min) and visible light (5 min), the emission intensities at 460 nm (from TPE) and at 650 nm (from MC) were obtained and plots against the irradiation cycles, as shown in Fig. 6
a and Figure S9
a, respectively. Note that all the intensities obtained at the same wavelength have been normalized to the corresponding maximum in the first cycle. Due to the relatively lower fluorescence intensity and signal-to-noise ratio of MC, the fluorescence intensity of MC fluctuated greatly in the 10 cycles. In order to better reflect the light fatigue resistance or fluorescence intensity recovery performance of PNPs, the fluorescence intensity of TPE, which is the more reliable, was mainly employed for this study. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the plot based on the TPE emission intensity at 460 nm, displays good reproducibility over 10 cycles for the reversible photoswitching. The fluorescence maxima were very stable except for the first cycle. However, interestingly, there is a gradual increase in the intensity minimum of TPE after each UV light irradiation after the 4th cycle. To explain this observation, the absorbance at 570 nm (from MC) was also recorded over 10 cycles of alternating irradiation of UV and visible lights ( Figure S10 ), and we found that the maximum absorbance in every cycle gradually decreased, decreasing by ∼ 20 % after the 10th UV irradiation. This phenomenon indicates that SP-MC conversion has a certain degree of fatigue under multiple UV light irradiation[44], resulting in a decrease in MC formation; and the increase in the minimum intensity of TPE per cycle in Fig. 6 a can be attributed to a decrease in MC absorbance and thus a decrease in FRET. The variation of MC emission intensity shown in Figure S9
a displays an opposite trend to that of TPE emission; this is consistent with the fact that FRET exists between TPE and MC. Overall, after 10 cycles of alternating irradiation of UV and visible lights, PMTS/PSMA PNPs still show significant photochromic properties and acceptable fatigue resistance.

Considering that 10 continuous verifications are usually not required in practical anti-counterfeiting applications but it is possible to repeat the verification twice a day, the PNPs in the colloidal solution are alternately irradiated with UV and visible lights for two cycles every day for five days, and the spectra are recorded. And the emission intensity was also for TPE and MC were also plot against the cycles, as shown in Fig. 6 b and S9b. Similar repeatability can be observed over five days, indicating that the reproducible photochromic properties are stable on different days.

Finally, the stability of PNPs was examined from two aspects, the fluorescence intensity stability of PNPs and the photochromic stability after storage. Specifically, the solution of PNPs was stored in the covered container in the dark under room temperature for 30 days. At days 0, 7, 14, 21, and 30, the solution was taken out and its fluorescence spectrum was recorded; and then after UV light irradiation, its fluorescence spectrum was recorded again. The fluorescence intensity at 460 nm before UV light irradiation on day 0 was used as a reference to normalize the fluorescence intensity before irradiation at different storage time to evaluate the stability of the fluorescence intensity after long-term storage. The stability of photochromism was evaluated by comparing the photoquenching efficiency (Eq) at different storage days, which was calculated based on the fluorescence intensity of the emission spectra at 460 nm before and after UV light irradiation. The specific calculation method was described previously in the experimental section. The histograms of normalized fluorescence intensity at 460 nm and Eq of PMTS/PSMA PNPs were plot against the number of storage days as in Fig. 6 c. The fluorescence intensity at 460 nm and the Eq, of the solution of PNPs in the covered container in the dark under room temperature, decreased by less than 5 % after 30 days of storage. Macroscopically, no colloidal aggregation or optical changes were observed under natural light after PMTS/PSMA PNPs were stored under ambient conditions for more than 6 months ( Figure S11 ). In all, PMTS/PSMA PNPs displayed good long-term stability.

The same investigation was performed on PMTS PNPs in colloidal solution. As shown in Figure S12
a and b, PMTS PNPs also showed stable reproducibility of photochromic behavior after 10 light cycles or 2 cycles for 5 days; however, compared with PMTS/PSMA PNPs, PMTS PNPs showed lower recovery of TPE fluorescence intensity after the same time of UV irradiation. Interestingly, as shown in Figure S12
c, the fluorescence intensity at 460 nm of PMTS PNPs significantly increased after the one month’s storage by about 20 % and the change in photoquenching efficiency is very slight (<5%). In addition, obvious aggregation and precipitation of PMTS PNPs was observed macroscopically after 6 months of storage ( Figure S13 ). Therefore, in the absence of PSMA as a stabilizing surfactant, PMTS PNPs is more likely to aggregate, resulting in the increase of fluorescence intensity, a typical phenomenon for AIE molecules.

In all, both PNPs have significant photochromic properties, acceptable fatigue resistance and acceptable stability in fluoresced intensity and photoquenching efficiency at 460 nm over long-time storage. However, during the photoswitching cycles, PMTS/PSMA PNPs has better recovery than PMTS PNPs. In addition, PMTS/PSMA PNPs have a higher photoquenching efficiency of 81.07 % on average, compared with PMTS PNPs (71 % on average). The main reason for these differences mentioned above may be attributed to that the amphiphilic surfactants makes PNPs uniformly dispersed in water, avoiding aggregation of nanoparticles, and thus improving the stability of PNPs when stored in water. In conclusion, for the study of photochromic behavior, the addition of PSMA allows the nanoparticles to possess superior fatigue resistance and long-term stability compared to the bare PMTS PNPs, making them ideal candidates for complex anti-counterfeiting applications.

Above studies show that PNPs have good reversible photochromic performance under alternating irradiation of UV and visible lights, and the FRET between TPE unit and MC unit was realized. With a very simple operation, the remarkable dynamic fluorescent color change of this material can be observed with the naked eyes. However, PNPs in colloidal solution cannot be used directly as inks for anti-counterfeiting applications. According to the literature[11,31], the aqueous solution of PNPs was concentrated using centrifugal ultrafiltration tubes, and a small amount of nonionic surfactant Triton X-100 was added in order to prevent the aggregation of PNPs during centrifugation. Then an appropriate amount of glycerol and ethanol were added to the concentrated solution of PNPs to prepare PNP ink. Note that glycerin can increase the viscosity of PNP ink, and ethanol can promote the solvent evaporation of PNP ink, making it dry as soon as possible. Next, the PMTS/PSMA PNP ink was employed for exploring anti-counterfeiting application in different scenarios.

The first scenario to is to drop PMTS/PSMA PNP ink evenly onto a piece of cellulose paper, which shows light yellow under natural light in both the initial state and after UV irradiation. ( Figure S14 ). Two modes of anti-counterfeiting can be envisioned. The simple one is directly using this doped paper as the encrypted label. Using a 365 nm UV light lamp as the tool, the color of the label is monitored, and if the color change is dynamic and similar to the colors shown in Fig. 7
a, the verification is accomplished. The more complicated mode is to couple this paper with a mask with hollow “SZ” pattern to form a double-layer anti-counterfeiting label. Here we employ decoding process similar to a previous report by our group[25], but the output color is different since we used different anti-counterfeiting material. With an operation mode for verification ( Figure S15 ), we can observe the different pattern/color combination as show in Fig. 7 b. Note that though the modes for verification here are similar to our previous report, there are two significant differences with respect to the material preparation and the output. First, compared with the previous electrospinning to obtain fiber film, this study is much simpler to drop ink on paper to obtain labels. Second, the fluorescence colors in different states in this study are different from our previous report.

The stability of the anti-counterfeiting effect was investigated in two ways. The first is to examine the stability of the handwriting against solvents by immersing cellulose paper with written “SOOCHOW” in different solvents for 1 h. Here three solvents were employed, with water and ethanol as the common polar solvents, and petroleum ether as a non-polar solvent. As shown in Figure S17
a and b, after immersion, the handwritten words remained almost unchanged, displaying similar photochromism effects and fluorescence intensity as the original ones before immersion. Second, to examine the storage stability, the paper with “SOOCHOW” on it was left under ambient conditions for 120 days without any solvent treatment. The photochromic effect is still very obvious, with fluorescence colors reversibly changing between blue and red under alternating irradiation of UV light and visible light ( Figure S17
c). In all, the words written by the ink were stable on cellulose paper under common ambient conditions.

With the rapid development of e-commerce recently, many online ordering goods need to be customized, so there is a great demand for providing individualized anti-counterfeiting labels. As shown above, fluorescence intensities/colors can be fine-tuned, label production methods and image pattens for encryption can be diversified, as well as different decoding/verification operations can be adopted. In terms of materials, the above two types of PNP ink can provide different intensities; in addition, the fluorescence color can also be tuned by mixing above dynamic photochromic PNP ink with other commercially available fluorescent inks to produce more diverse inks. For the encryption process, as long as we have ink, paper and brush/pen at hand, we can easily make various styles of anti-counterfeiting labels to meet the individual needs of the e-commerce field; especially for those small business owners can customize the patterns on a one-to-one basis according to the customers’ requirements. Last, for the decoding process, different power of UV light and visible light will result in different output, such as different photochromic conversion times of the and different fluorescence intensities, as preliminary demonstrated by the use 60 W or 12 W UV light. Therefore, from the manufacture of anti-counterfeiting label to decryption/verification, by varying the combination ink, encryption mode/pattern and the verification equipment and process, a series of anti-counterfeiting strategies can be envisioned.

To develop new fluorescent materials based on dynamic photochromism and FRET for anti-counterfeiting applications, the terpolymer PMMA-TPE-SP was designed and successfully synthesized. Photophysical studies of the polymer in solution showed a transition from SP to MC under UV irradiation and an AIE effect in mixed solvents. Then PMTS PNPs and PMTS/PSMA PNPs, without and with additional addition of surfactant PSMA into the PMMA-TPE-SP solution, respectively, were successfully prepared by reprecipitation method. The successful combination of photochromism and FRET was demonstrated based on the emission spectra of the PNPs; the dynamic fluorescence color-change was visible to the naked eye after UV light irradiation, and the process was reversible under visible light irradiation. PNPs was found to have the good fatigue resistance which is favorable for multiple verifications for anti-counterfeiting; in addition, they also have good stability of fluorescence intensity and photoquenching efficiency at 460 nm in long-term storage. The ink prepared with PMTS/PSMA PNPs were then used in several anti-counterfeiting scenarios with desirable dynamic fluorescence response and stability on cellulose. The preparation of photoswitching fluorescent anti-counterfeiting encryption materials using PNP ink is an efficient, simple and customizable method. The aqueous PNP ink can be immediately used for rewritable printing, rewritable data storage or for making security label and is expected to have good prospects for practical anti-counterfeiting applications.

Yuchen Weng: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ying Hong: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Jingyu Deng: Data curation. Sicheng Cao: Data curation. Li-Juan Fan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation, Writing – review & editing.
